summary: This notebook demonstrates a conceptual, multi-modal pipeline for monitoring cognitive health by fusing text, audio, and visual inputs. It simulates linguistic, acoustic, and computer vision engines to compute a weighted composite score, which is then translated into a clinical MMSE equivalent and risk level. The author showcases how explainability tools, longitudinal tracking, and population distribution plots can be integrated into a diagnostic workflow. Readers should understand how multi-modal feature fusion and threshold-based alerting can structure a clinical AI system, even without real model training.
competition_flow: Simulates text, audio, and image inputs through custom engines to compute a weighted cognitive health score, maps it to an MMSE equivalent, and generates clinical reports, visualizations, and validation charts.
features_engineering: Computes derived metrics such as (1.0 - pause_frequency) for acoustic scoring, uses hardcoded semantic_density_score and vision_score, and calculates a weighted composite score (0.4 linguistic, 0.3 acoustic, 0.3 vision).
Maps the composite score to an MMSE scale (0-30) and categorical dementia stages for clinical translation.
ensembling: Fuses three simulated modalities using a fixed weighted average (linguistic 0.4, acoustic 0.3, vision 0.3) to produce a single composite cognitive score.
insights: Multi-modal fusion of linguistic, acoustic, and visual-spatial features provides a more robust cognitive assessment than single-modality approaches.
Threshold-based alerting and MMSE mapping bridge AI outputs with clinical decision-making.
Explainability tools like risk-highlighted text and radar charts improve interpretability for medical stakeholders.
Longitudinal tracking and population distribution plots contextualize individual patient scores within broader clinical cohorts.
